next_steps: Keep access:ff238-public-digital-search at no_public_copy_found dated 2026-07-17.
Update only if CUL responds with an existing digital object or supplies a complete reproduction.
openQuestions.question: Does CUL already have a complete digital facsimile of Ff.2.38, or will it supply a complete reproduction?

next_steps: Do not put future CUL-supplied files in Git or publish them unless CUL grants express written permission covering that use.
Keep research conclusions derived from consultation distinct from redistribution of the facsimile itself.
openQuestions.question: What exact reuse rights, if any, will CUL grant for image files supplied in response to the pending request?

next_steps: Use ff.161r-239v for the target locus.
Retain the preface wording as an apparent forty-folio typographical or catalog error, not a second target locus.
openQuestions.question: Why does Zupitza's preface state current ff.121-239 / old ff.107-225 when the text and milestones begin at f.161?

next_steps: Preserve the conflict.
Use Wiggins and Zupitza for the complete boundary unless DIMEV clarifies the record.
openQuestions.question: Why does DIMEV record 4908 combine a whole-redaction 11,976-line extent with a Guy-only terminal locator and last lines?

next_steps: Do not infer that ff.231v-239v are absent or a separate work.
Retain Wiggins's codicological and structural argument that the continuation is integral.
openQuestions.question: Why does D-MER list Guy only through f.231r and then a later item at f.239v?

next_steps: Retain Wiggins's detailed pre-1400 conclusion at medium confidence.
Preserve D-MER's early-fifteenth-century claim as an attributed alternative.
openQuestions.question: Should future scholarship prefer a pre-1400 or early-fifteenth-century date for the Later Couplet redaction?

next_steps: Record two serial numbers or parts without assigning an exact split.
Inspect an original wrapper or bibliographic collation if the precise boundary becomes material.
openQuestions.question: Where did the original EETS Extra Series 25 volume end and number 26 begin?

next_steps: Keep Caius as a supporting witness with exact textual ranges and no local facsimile.
Recheck the holding institution separately if images become necessary.
openQuestions.question: Is a complete public digital facsimile of Gonville and Caius College MS 107/176 available?

next_steps: Do not claim the supplemental files are cached.
Recheck the repository or contact it if those appendices become necessary.
notes: Checked direct bitstream URLs returned 404 or timed out.
openQuestions.question: Can the Appendix C transcription and Appendix J edition-review supplements advertised by the Wiggins repository record be recovered?

next_steps: Preserve both provider statements as separate time- and component-scoped rights records.
Do not silently treat one as erasing the other.
openQuestions.question: How should the current U-M public-domain/NoC-US item statement be reconciled with the generic 2006 raw-header warning?

next_steps: Do not infer that the license file alone resolves XML-content rights.
Seek an explicit project statement if a broad redistribution claim is needed.
openQuestions.question: Does the software-focused Unlicense wording in the pinned CME repository apply to XML text, data, and markup?

Supporting relation IDs: relation:ia-package-contains-raw; relation:ia-package-contains-tiffs; relation:ia-scan-facsimile-of-zupitza; relation:ia-ocr-encoded-from-scan; relation:zupitza-contains-later-couplet; relation:zupitza-collated-against-caius; relation:caius-contains-later-couplet-passages; relation:mcsparran-facsimile-of-ff238; relation:dimev-describes-work; relation:dimev-describes-ff238; relation:dimev-describes-caius; relation:dmer-describes-work; relation:dmer-describes-ff238; relation:cul1857-describes-ff238; relation:wiggins-describes-work; relation:wiggins-describes-ff238; relation:wiggins-describes-zupitza; relation:dixon-describes-ff238.
The cataloging boundary is one 11,976-line work. Guy lines 1-10,786 and the Harrawde/Reinbrun continuation lines 10,787-11,976 are analytical movements, not separately counted works.
Printed p.310 is shared by the two analytical movements. In Ff.2.38, Guy ends on f.231ra, f.231rb is blank, and the continuation begins on f.231va.
DIMEV record 4908 gives a whole-redaction extent but a Guy-only terminal locator; D-MER likewise omits the continuation from its Ff.2.38 item list. Wiggins and Zupitza control the complete boundary.

Access and rights attach to named layers. Public-domain medieval text does not automatically confer reuse rights in modern photographs, interfaces, encodings, scholarship, or future CUL-supplied files.

Carrier-mediated rights access stays on the same entity as the rights record: the raw encoding is reached through artifact:internet-archive:anz4364-umich-source-package, and the Zupitza edition is reached through facsimile:internet-archive:zupitza-1875-1876.

lineage.summary: The General Corpus XML is a normalized copy of the University of Michigan CME encoding ANZ4364.0001.001, keyed from a complete 482-TIFF capture of Julius Zupitza's 1875-1876 EETS edition. Zupitza transcribes Cambridge University Library MS Ff.2.38 as principal text and collates only two exact line ranges against Gonville and Caius College MS 107/176. Ff.2.38 contains the complete 11,976-line Later Couplet redaction, including the integral Harrawde/Reinbrun continuation. Complete U-M package and independent Zupitza edition scans are cached outside Git; no verified public complete digital facsimile of Ff.2.38 or Caius MS 107/176 was found, and a CUL whole-manuscript reproduction enquiry is pending.

catalogingSubject.label: Guy of Warwick (Later Couplet version)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: One continuous 11,976-line Middle English romance redaction, including the Guy narrative through line 10,786 and its integral Harrawde/Reinbrun continuation at lines 10,787-11,976. The two movements are nested analytical parts, not separately counted works. Modern edition front matter, notes, glossary/index matter, page-image navigation, and physical manuscript carriers remain metadata or lineage layers.

locators.scheme: XML nodes and direct poem lines

contentParts.summary: Analytical movement through Guy's conclusion and the explicit ending at lines 10,783-10,786; it is not a separately counted work.

contentParts.label: Harrawde and Reinbrun continuation

locators.scheme: XML nodes and direct poem lines

locators.value: 231va-239v; final line on f.239va, first column

notes: Printed p.310 is shared with the preceding movement; XML node 38 begins after the p.310 PB element and therefore first lists p.311 even though its opening lines are physically on p.310.
Wiggins argues from layout and codicology that the continuation is integral to the same redaction rather than an independent work.
contentParts.summary: Analytical movement beginning 'Lystenyth now' and carrying the integral narrative continuation through the final Amen; it is not a separately counted work.

notes: Wiggins dates all Middle English redaction archetypes before the end of the fourteenth century. D-MER instead calls the Later Couplet version early fifteenth century; preserve that as a conflict rather than silently combining the dates.
The surviving Ff.2.38 copy is late fifteenth or early sixteenth century and must not be substituted for the redaction date.

extent.description: One verse work comprising 11,976 direct body poem lines in 40 direct body DIV1/LG groups. Sixteen L elements nested in editorial notes are excluded. The two nested analytical movements partition the direct poem lines and groups but have item_count null, so they do not inflate numbered_items=1.

formStatements.description: An 11,976-line Middle English romance in short rhyming couplets; prose edition matter and note quotations are excluded from the work boundary.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The inherited U-M header code eng describes the modern delivery broadly; the medieval work text is Middle English.
Treat the record as one work with two nested analytical movements; do not split the continuation into a second counted item.
The 40 direct body DIV1/LG groups are encoding and edition-navigation units, not 40 independent works and not a proved stanza count.

The source edition was issued as EETS Extra Series numbers 25 and 26 in 1875-1876. The exact original serial-part page boundary has not been verified and must not be invented from the later bound scan.
The source edition's title calls this the second or fifteenth-century version, but the surviving witness date and the redaction's proposed composition date are separate statements.

next_steps: Retain Wiggins's detailed pre-1400 conclusion at medium confidence.
Preserve D-MER's early-fifteenth-century statement as an attributed conflict and review future scholarship.
openQuestions.question: Should future scholarship prefer a fourteenth-century or early-fifteenth-century date for the Later Couplet redaction?

next_steps: Keep a work/tradition-level adaptation relation rather than asserting a manuscript-level French source path.
Review future stemmatic or source studies for an exact exemplar claim.
openQuestions.question: Which Anglo-Norman textual state or physical exemplar underlies this Middle English redaction?

next_steps: Record two serial parts without assigning an unverified exact page boundary.
Inspect original wrappers or a bibliographic collation if the serial boundary becomes material.
openQuestions.question: Where did the original EETS Extra Series 25 volume end and number 26 begin?

notes: Wiggins localizes the E-redaction archetype linguistically to the Lincoln/Kesteven-Holland area and concludes South Lincolnshire is probable.
This is a work/redaction-level linguistic inference. Do not transfer the surviving witness's production dialect or present Cambridge location to composition metadata.

subjects.label: Penance, pilgrimage, religious spaces, and sacrament
Marriage, friendship, mistaken identity, recognition tokens, and succession through Reinbrun

summaries.value: The fullest continuous Middle English couplet redaction of Guy of Warwick traces Guy's chivalric career, exile, return, penance, and death, then continues integrally with the fortunes of his son Reinbrun and the associated Harrawde narrative. The surviving Ff.2.38 witness and Zupitza's edition present the continuation as part of one work despite a strong internal codicological and typographic boundary.

The record covers one 11,976-line redaction. Guy lines 1-10,786 and the Harrawde/Reinbrun continuation lines 10,787-11,976 are nested analytical movements with item_count null. Printed p.310 is shared across their boundary, whereas the manuscript ends the first movement on f.231ra, leaves f.231rb blank, and begins the continuation on f.231va.

notes: Later Couplet is retained as a redaction label; the parenthetical qualifier distinguishes this work from other Middle English Guy of Warwick versions.
This is the source-edition title, not an independent medieval title or a current composition-date claim.

notes: Use only with the redaction qualifier where confusion with other Middle English versions is possible.

work.summary: The fullest continuous Middle English couplet redaction of Guy of Warwick traces Guy's chivalric career, exile, return, penance, and death, then continues integrally with the fortunes of his son Reinbrun and the associated Harrawde narrative. The surviving Ff.2.38 witness and Zupitza's edition present the continuation as part of one work despite a strong internal codicological and typographic boundary.
